title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Value of Higher Education in the Digital Age

texts.A: The rising cost of a university degree is becoming harder to justify in a world where the world's best lectures are available for free online. [40]We are clinging to a medieval model of 'the campus' that was designed for an era of information scarcity.[/40] [37]For most professions, a six-month intensive 'boot camp' followed by an apprenticeship would be far more effective than four years of academic theory.[/37] [39]The university has become a 'credential mill'—a way for employers to filter candidates based on social class rather than actual ability.[/39]
texts.B: [38]University is about more than just 'job training'; it is the only time in a person's life dedicated to the development of critical thinking and the exploration of diverse ideas.[/38] [40]To reduce education to its 'economic utility' is to miss the point of human flourishing.[/40] I agree that the debt crisis is a major issue, but the solution is public funding, not the destruction of the institution. A society without a broad-based liberal arts education is a society that is vulnerable to demagoguery and narrow-mindedness.
texts.C: [39]I share the view that the 'credentialism' of modern hiring is a problem, but I disagree that online courses are a replacement for the university experience.[/39] Learning is a social process. [38]The informal networks, the peer-to-peer debates, and the mentorship of a professor cannot be replicated by a pre-recorded video.[/38] However, we must reform the curriculum. We need a 'hybrid' model that combines the depth of traditional academic inquiry with the practical, data-driven skills required by the modern economy.
texts.D: The current debate over 'theory versus practice' is a distraction. The real value of a degree is its signal of 'grit'—the ability to complete a difficult, long-term project. While I share the skepticism regarding the current price tag, [37]I believe the 'academic theory' that critics dismiss is actually what allows workers to be adaptable.[/37] If you only learn a specific 'skill' in a boot camp, you are obsolete as soon as the technology changes. A theoretical grounding allows you to learn how to learn.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the value of 'academic theory'?
questions.explanation: Expert A dismisses academic theory in favor of 'boot camps.' Expert D argues that academic theory is exactly what makes workers 'adaptable' and helps them 'learn how to learn.'

questions.question: Which expert shares C's opinion about the social dimension of learning?
questions.explanation: Expert C says learning is a 'social process' and mentions 'peer-to-peer debates.' Expert B also emphasizes the 'exploration of diverse ideas' and critical thinking that happens within the university community.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to A regarding the 'credential' function of degrees?
questions.explanation: Expert A calls university a 'credential mill.' Expert C shares the view that 'credentialism' in modern hiring is a problem.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the primary purpose of a university education?
questions.explanation: Expert B believes the purpose is 'human flourishing' and 'critical thinking.' Expert A sees it primarily as an outdated and inefficient model of preparation for work that should be replaced by practical training.
